title: Life is Strange Remastered
summary: Worse than the original in every way
body:
Just buy the original. Graphics are really a side grade at best and bugs were added that the original doesn't have.

[url=https://store.steampowered.com/app/319630/Life_is_Strange__Episode_1/]The Original can be found here.[/url]

title: Life is Strange: Before the Storm Remastered
summary: A decent attempt at recreating the secret formula of Life is Strange, ruined by being overly dramatic and fearing the fans' canon expectations.
body:
[b]If you choose to play the game please BUY THE ORIGINAL. Remakes are always a scam in my opinion but deck 9 remakes really take the cake. Subtitles constantly  freeze, audio lines don't actually play all the time, and model lighting was janky in multiple scenes.[/b]

Now as for the game itself, I'm just kind of angry having finished it moments ago. How in the world was I more moved emotionally by the 120 second montage of random moments and a cell phone on a table then the entirety of the game!?!? 

 They did all the world stuff that Life is Strange did, and surprise surprise it all worked out pretty good. 

However everything else was pretty bad. 

The choices and characters are made irrelevant because the game "had to" stay true to the cannon. 

The plot as a whole was fineish but the execution was just a crappy soap opera. 

Overall the game as a whole really ended up being the Rachel Amber story instead of Rachel x Chloe story which is what it should have been. Really don't recommend if you weren't a fan of the original and even then it's main appeal is some believable Chloe moments/dialogue.

tabs.name: The Good, The Bad, & The Okay
tabs.content:
[h1]The Good[/h1][list]
[*] Graffiti Collectables
[*] Journal Entries
[*] Chloe's Backtalk Mini Game
[*] Chloe was a Believable Chloe
[*] Current Objective Drawn on Hand
[*] Music
[/list]
[h1]The Bad[/h1][list]
[*]Most of The Adult Voice Actors, especially Amber's parents
[*]Most Scenes With Damon 
[*]3 Day Timeline For Entire Game?!
[*]Eliot, just all things Eliot 
[/list]
[h1]The Okay[/h1][list]
[*] Dead Dad Visions
[*] Choices Barely Matter
[*] Backgrounds Depicted of the Characters from the Original
[/list]

tabs.name: Game Mechanics
tabs.content:
[tab=Backtalk]
[h1]What Is The Mechanic[/h1]Using context from the dialogue of the person talking to you give a response that is related, generally in the form of a relevant diss. 

[h1]Thoughts[/h1]I really quite enjoyed this mini game. Paying attention to the character's words and finding the relevant option in a time crunch felt really satisfying.
[/tab]

[tab=Graffiti]
[h1]What Is The Mechanic[/h1]Find places throughout the world where you vandalize property in a generally humorous manner. 

[h1]Thoughts[/h1]I was impressed with how believable this form of collectable was for Chloe and liked having a choice(in most cases) of graffiti. I looked for these every chance I had and honestly liked them more than the photo collectables of the original Life is Strange.
[/tab]

title: Life is Strange™
summary: A classic for a reason. Interesting story and good characters, with a super power twist.
body: Life is Strange at its core is really just a good story. You could remove the powers, choices, and attachments to the characters while still having a solid story.  Once all those things are added on to that stable foundation you really get something special.

tabs.name: The Good, The Bad, & The Okay
tabs.content:
[h1]The Good[/h1][list]
[*] Great Story
[*] Complex Characters with thought out backgrounds
[*] Unique Hand Crafted Menus and interface prompts
[*] Community Choice Comparisons
[*]Memorable Music
[*]Solid Voice Acting
[/list]
[h1]The Bad[/h1][list]
[*]Episodic Releases
[/list]
[h1]The Okay[/h1][list][*] Choices Barely Matter, generally resulted in dialogue changes but not actual story changes. While the character's feelings towards Max were altered the overarching story remained the same except for 2 choices.
[*]Time Manipulation Powers, they serve their purpose narratively but felt a bit flat from a gameplay perspective
[/list]

tabs.name: Game Mechanics
tabs.content:
[tab=Time Rewind]
[h1]What Is The Mechanic[/h1]Player keeps inventory and position on map but the local events are rewound

[h1]How I Think This Works[/h1]My first thoughts were that everything would be tracked and therefore you just rewind literally everything on the screen however much time has passed.

Thinking about it more this is clearly not the case since the amount of time doesn't matter. In fact the features of Max's rewind power kind of confirm all this since the game doesn't reset your position or inventory. 

So instead of rewinding the timeline I believe that each map has local events to trigger and after you trigger them it is added to the rewind bar, so when  you rewind it references that event and resets it once you rewind past it.

Upon further review it looks like a combination. When you reach an event it saves a checkpoint of sorts, then the rewind bar rewinds up to the max duration of that event. Most characters on the scene are on loop but one event runs for a duration before hitting an end point that is either off screen or a loop once again. When you use rewind it rewinds from the end point up to the start point.
[/tab]

[tab=Point & Click]
[h1]What Is The Mechanic[/h1]When you get close to an interactable object text appears on the screen and a button can be pressed to interact with it.

[h1]How I Think This Works[/h1]Interacable objects have two boundaries around them that do things when a character crosses the edge.

The outer boundary makes text appear on the screen that serves as a label for the object. The inner boundary brings up the interacting ui so you can do something with that object, whether that be picking up, moving, or just getting information about.
[/tab]

tabs.name: Thoughts
tabs.content:
[tab=Time Powers]
The time powers in this game were a very convenient way to see all the possible branches of a conversation and/or decision. I did appreciate being able to check the other possible localized outcomes even if they didn't cause the craziest changes to the story. 

The rewinding puzzles while cool in concept were rather flat feeling since there was only 1 solution and you had to find it to progress the story. I will say that the solutions always threw me for a loop mentally since the game expected you to treat the rewind as essentially a teleport even though it makes perfect logical sense I had to double take every time.

The photo hopping ability was also really cool and essential to the story at the end but once again they felt a bit flat since Max always ended up at the same path despite the hops. 

Overall the time powers are only okay, they serve their purpose narratively but felt a bit flat from a gameplay perspective.
[/tab]

[tab=Characters]
The characters in this game are good because they aren't one dimensional and written with care. Don't get me wrong there are plenty of one dimensional characters but they are side characters to help fill out the setting. Saving Alyssa every chapter was a highlight of every act for me, Warren is the nerd that goes from zero to hero, and many of the other side characters had their bits that helped flesh everything out. Were they complex? No, but they did their part and were enjoyable to experience. 

As for the main characters they are what I meant by not one dimensional:
[list]
[*] Chloe: The best friend and potential love interest, has a traumatic past that really shows with her actions in the story. While she clearly cares about Max, many of her actions are selfish and actively put them both in danger. She's kind of an asshole but it really does make sense for her to be one.
[*]Victoria: The school queen and rich kid snob, serves as an antagonist for Max but can also show she's human on several different occasions.
[*]Nathan: even this absolute asshole is humanized by the end of the game.
[/list]
Honestly not gonna go over every character, in the end all of them are given enough attention to fill their role and feel realistic enough to enjoy the story.
[/tab]

[tab=LGBTQ]
For better or worse, Life is Strange has been claimed by the LGBTQ community for the relationship that can develop between Max & Chloe. While this does exist as an option I believe this is just another cherry on top. 

The intimate portions of this game comes down to two scenes, one of which is a dare and the other a tragic farewell at the end of the game. All the other choices can be interpreted as just two people that truly care about each other and enjoy being in each other's company. While the characters clearly love and care about each other it doesn't mean it has to because they are physically attracted to one another.

To shoehorn the story into specifically LGBTQ is a disservice to the game. It's always rubbed me the wrong way that LGBTQ has so strongly expressed their sexuality to the point it really feels like it is their identity, isn't the goal to normalize that part of who you are rather than highlight it as something unique and special.

Anyways I digress, at the end of the day I personally believe the story is just as, if not more impactful, as two close friends rather than star crossed lovers.
[/tab]

[tab=Endings]
When I first played this game a decade ago I made the "logical" and "morally right" decision to save Arcadia Bay, while respecting Chloe's wishes to let her sacrifice herself. The story constantly hints at the fact that what has happened is meant to be and to go against it is going to cause failure in some way or another. And to this day I do think this particular ending makes an objectively better and complete story. The despair and hopelessness of not being able to really change anything despite knowing what could be if you did is truly heart wrenching. 

However, when I played it the second time 10 years later I refused to sacrifice Chloe. I honestly didn't feel responsible for the town and I placed much more importance on what I could live with. The people in the city have their own agency and making the sacrifice is literally putting it all on your shoulders.  Is this selfish? Absolutely. Is this a bad thing? I'm not so sure anymore. My default nowadays is to think of myself before others. To act in a way that you believe is best for everyone while expecting them to do the same is a quick road to disappointment and frustration.

Anyways,  I just find it interesting that all these years later I have completely switched sides. There's a reason the ending has been such a heavily debated choice for the fanbase and yet another reason the game has made itself an icon of story games.
[/tab]
